gül-dölen.html.title: Gül Dölen
gül-dölen.html.description:
Gül Dölen is an Associate Professor of Psychology and Neuroscience at UC Berkeley (formerly at Johns Hopkins University). Her research investigates the neural mechanisms of social behavior and critical periods for synaptic plasticity. Social-Reward and the role of oxytocin. Psychedelics and the reopening of critical periods for social learning.
Synaptic plasticity in neurodevelopmental disorders (Fragile-X, autism).
Dölen-lab-JHU (formerly; now UC Berkeley) UC Berkeley Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine (Former)

liqun-luo.html.description:
Liqun Luo is the Ann and Bill Swindells Professor in the School of Humanities and Sciences and Professor of Biology at Stanford University. His research focuses on the assembly and organization of neural circuits. Mapping neural circuits using genetic tools (MARCM, viral tracers). Principles of olfactory system organization in flies and mice. Role of activity in circuit development.

joseph-g-gleeson.html.description:
Joseph G. Gleeson is the Rady Professor of Neuroscience at UC San Diego and Director of Neuroscience at the Rady Children's Institute for Genomic Medicine. His research focuses on identifying the genetic causes of neurodevelopmental disorders, particularly autism and epilepsy. PI of Gleeson-lab-UCSD. Genomic discovery of recessive neurodevelopmental disorders. Mechanisms of brain development and cortical malformations.
Role of the nBAF-complex in autism Wenderski2020_actl6b-autism.
Gleeson-lab-UCSD UC San Diego School of Medicine Howard Hughes Medical Institute (HHMI)

allyson-k-friedman.html.title: Allyson K. Friedman
allyson-k-friedman.html.description:
Associate Professor at Hunter College, CUNY (formerly at Mount Sinai), known for her research on homeostatic mechanisms of resilience in midbrain dopamine neurons. Trained in the Han-lab-Mount-Sinai.

scott-j-russo.html.title: Scott J. Russo
scott-j-russo.html.description:
Professor of Neuroscience and Director of the Center for Affective Neuroscience at the Friedman Brain Institute, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, known for research on the neural and immunological mechanisms of stress and aggression. PI of Russo-lab-Mount-Sinai; PhD mentor to Dan-J-Christoffel.
Senior author on Christoffel2011_IkBkinase-social-defeat — IκB kinase as NAc regulator of CSDS-induced depressive behavior (Christoffel's first paper).
Senior author on Christoffel2012_IkBkinase-NAc-emotional — IκB kinase in NAc gating of emotional behavior.
Senior author on Hodes2014_peripheral-immune-resilience — peripheral immune mechanisms of stress resilience.
Senior author on Hodes2015_sex-differences-NAc-transcriptome — sex differences in NAc transcriptome response to stress.
Senior author on Golden2016_BF-lHb-aggression — established reward valence of aggression.
Senior author on Christoffel2015_thalamostriatal-stress — thalamostriatal CSDS susceptibility.
Co-author on Chaudhury2013_VTA-firing.
Co-author on Walsh2014_CRF-BDNF-mesolimbic.
Co-author on Heller2014_Fosb-ZFP-epigenetic.
Principal Investigator of Russo-lab-Mount-Sinai.

ming-hu-han.html.title: Ming-Hu Han
ming-hu-han.html.description:
Professor of Pharmacological Sciences and Neuroscience at the Friedman Brain Institute, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, known for pioneering work on the neurophysiology of resilience and VTA circuit specificity in depression. PI of Han-lab-Mount-Sinai. PhD mentor to Jessica-J-Walsh.
Senior author on Cao2010_mesolimbic-DA-susceptibility — phasic VTA firing pattern as susceptibility marker.
Senior author on Chaudhury2013_VTA-firing — identified phasic VTA firing as a driver of depression.
Senior author on Walsh2014_CRF-BDNF-mesolimbic — CRF-BDNF gate in mesolimbic reward pathway.
Senior author on Walsh2014_VTA-heterogeneity-review — review of VTA heterogeneity in mood.
Senior author on Friedman2014_homeostatic-resilience — discovered homeostatic mechanisms of resilience.
Senior author on Golden2016_BF-lHb-aggression — identified subcortical circuits for aggression reward.
Co-author on Christoffel2011_IkBkinase-social-defeat.
Co-author on Heller2014_Fosb-ZFP-epigenetic.
Co-author on Christoffel2015_thalamostriatal-stress.
Co-author on Koo2016_mesolimbic-BDNF-stress.
Principal Investigator of Han-lab-Mount-Sinai.

robert-c-malenka.html.title: Robert C. Malenka
robert-c-malenka.html.description:
Nancy Pritzker Professor in Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences at Stanford University School of Medicine. A pioneer in the study of synaptic plasticity and the neural mechanisms of drug addiction and neuropsychiatric disorders. PI of Malenka-lab-Stanford. Molecular mechanisms of long-term potentiation (LTP) and long-term depression (LTD).
Neural circuitry of the nucleus-accumbens (NAc) and the mesolimbic reward system.
Modulation of social behavior by oxytocin, serotonin, and dopamine.
Pathophysiology of autism-spectrum-disorder (ASD) and addiction.
Former Postdoc Mentor to Jessica-J-Walsh.
Former Postdoc Mentor to Dan-J-Christoffel.

jessica-j-walsh.html.title: Jessica J. Walsh
jessica-j-walsh.html.description:
PI of Walsh-lab-UNC (UNC Chapel Hill, Dept of Pharmacology; Genetic Medicine Building) since July 2021. Spouse and frequent collaborator of Dan-J-Christoffel (PI of Christoffel-lab-UNC). B.A. Neuroscience & Behavior, Columbia College, Columbia University (2008). Undergrad lab: Gerald Fischbach (Dept of Pharmacology). Began in the Fischbach lab as a high-school student. Research Coordinator, Columbia University Dept of Pharmacology (05/2008–08/2008). M.S. Biomedical Sciences, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai (2010). Research Coordinator, Mt. Sinai Dept of Neuroscience (05/2010–08/2010).
Ph.D. Neuroscience, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai (08/2013). Mentor: Ming-Hu-Han (Han-lab-Mount-Sinai).
Postdoctoral Fellow, Stanford University (10/2013–06/2021, ~8 years). Mentor: Robert-C-Malenka (Malenka-lab-Stanford). Assistant Professor of Pharmacology, UNC Chapel Hill (07/2021–present).
Walsh's program centers on the brain circuits and molecular mechanisms governing motivated behavior, with major emphasis on autism-spectrum-disorder (ASD). She uses a systems-level approach combining well-defined ASD mouse models (e.g., 16p11.2 deletion, Cntnap2 knockout, Fmr1 knockout), in vivo and slice electrophysiology, optogenetics, and pharmacology to map circuit-level mechanisms of sociability and identify novel pharmacological targets (e.g., 5-HT system, 5-HT1b-receptor). She emphasizes a translational arc: define causal circuits → deep-characterize their physiology → target specific receptors that might be therapeutically tractable for ASD social symptoms. The independent UNC program builds on (a) her Han-lab-Mount-Sinai PhD work on midbrain DA / VTA circuitry in stress susceptibility/resilience and (b) her Malenka-lab-Stanford postdoc work on NAc serotonergic prosocial mechanisms and ASD circuit rescue. F31 (NRSA pre-doctoral) — NIMH, 2012. F32 MH103949 (Walsh, PI) — NIMH, 07/01/14–11/30/16. Title: "Systems level investigation of di-synaptic circuit involved in panic disorder." Project pivoted mid-fellowship from a panic-disorder LH/A2 circuit to the dorsal raphe (DR) basis of autism-like social deficits after the initial aim proved unfeasible. A.P. Giannini Foundation Postdoctoral Fellowship — declined (2014). Sammy Kuo Award in Neuroscience — Stanford best-postdoc-publication (2018). Hausfeld Award in Neuroscience — Mt. Sinai best senior grad student (2012). FENS/IBRO-PERC Travel Grant (Copenhagen, 2016). Multiple SfN travel awards. Notable speaking roles: SfN Nanosymposium Organizer/Chair/Speaker (2012 New Orleans, 2013 San Diego); 11th Annual Autism Spectrum Disorders Update (2018, Stanford); first-place poster at Mt. Sinai Neuroscience Retreat (2011).
NCBI bibliography: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/myncbi/jessica.walsh.1/bibliography/public/
Fischbach (undergrad, Columbia) → Ming-Hu-Han (PhD, Mt. Sinai Pharmacology & Systems Therapeutics) → Robert-C-Malenka (postdoc, Stanford Psychiatry & Behavioral Sciences) → PI at UNC Pharmacology.Slice and in vivo electrophysiology + optogenetics + viral pathway tracing across all three career eras. Added 5-HT pharmacology, MDMA, and ASD-model behavior at Stanford. Now extends at UNC into ASD circuit dissection (Cntnap2, 16p11.2) and 5-HT1B-targeted therapeutics.

abram-amsel.html.title: Abram Amsel
abram-amsel.html.description:
American experimental psychologist; foundational figure in the study of frustrative nonreward. Developed Amsel-frustration-theory and the double-runway paradigm in rats.
Key papers (cited in Naik2024_APRO_FNR_juvenile_mice): Amsel & Roussel (1952). J Exp Psychol. Amsel & Ward (1954). J Exp Psychol. Amsel & Hancock (1957). J Exp Psychol. Amsel (1958). Psychol Bull.
Theoretical scaffold for APRO-paradigm and the construct of frustrative-nonreward.

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.
